skill.name: ssh
skill.description: A Markdown reference for SSH connections, key management, file transfer, tunneling, jump hosts, agent setup, and troubleshooting commands.
skill.summary: Use SSH command patterns for remote access, secure transfers, tunnels, and key workflows.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution hits, but these are Markdown code examples rather than executable skill code. The skill is still medium risk because it teaches commands that modify SSH files, load private keys into an agent, enable forwarding, and create tunnels, which require user review before use.

medium_findings.title: SSH Key and Agent Operations Require User Review
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill includes examples for selecting SSH identity files, copying public keys, starting ssh-agent, and adding private keys to the agent. These are legitimate SSH workflows, but they interact with sensitive authentication material and should not be executed without explicit user intent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples directly reference SSH identity files and private key loading. The context is instructional rather than malicious, so the risk is operational and credential-sensitive rather than a confirmed attack.

medium_findings.title: Forwarding and SOCKS Proxy Examples Can Bypass Network Boundaries
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill documents local forwarding, remote forwarding, dynamic SOCKS proxying, jump hosts, and agent forwarding. These features are legitimate administration tools, but they can expose local services or route traffic through another host if used incorrectly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly enable forwarding behavior, including ForwardAgent and a SOCKS5 proxy. The commands are normal SSH usage, but the network-control impact is clear.

medium_findings.title: Known Hosts Update Without Verification Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill shows ssh-keyscan appending directly to known_hosts. This can be useful, but without fingerprint verification it may train users to trust an unverified host key.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command writes discovered host key data into the SSH trust store. No surrounding text explains independent fingerprint verification, so the concern is specific and evidence-based.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static analysis treated Markdown command examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. SKILL.md contains documentation snippets, not executable Ruby code or scripts that run automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown and the matched lines are fenced command examples. I found no executable source file, hook, or automation that would run these commands by itself.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Private Network Addresses Are Documentation Placeholders
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static analysis flagged private IP addresses in example SSH config blocks. The addresses are RFC1918-style placeholders used to illustrate HostName configuration, not external exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both IP addresses appear inside example configuration snippets. No evidence found of code that contacts these addresses automatically or sends data to them.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hit Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The analyzer reported weak cryptography at the front matter description, but the visible content recommends Ed25519 and labels RSA as legacy compatibility. No confirmed weak default was found at the reported location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reported line contains descriptive metadata, not an algorithm selection. The later RSA example is explicitly marked legacy compatibility, while Ed25519 is recommended.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive SSH File Paths in Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation references files under ~/.ssh, including identity files, config, sockets, and known_hosts. This is expected for an SSH skill, but these paths are sensitive and should be edited only with clear user approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly name SSH configuration, key, socket, and trust-store paths. The concern is inherent to SSH administration workflows.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Command and Transfer Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes remote command execution and file transfer examples using ssh, scp, and rsync. Users should review hostnames and paths before running them because these commands affect remote systems and local files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples clearly perform remote execution and bidirectional file transfer. The file is documentation, so the pattern is dangerous only if copied and run without review.

dangerous_patterns.title: Prompt Injection Not Found
dangerous_patterns.description: No evidence found of text that claims to override system instructions, skip security review, or impersonate higher-priority instructions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: I reviewed the complete Markdown file and did not find prompt injection language. The content is ordinary SSH reference material.

content.value_statement: SSH work often requires exact command syntax and careful handling of keys. This skill provides practical patterns for connections, transfers, tunnels, jump hosts, and troubleshooting.
actual_capabilities: Shows basic SSH connection patterns, including custom ports and identity files.
Provides SSH config examples for hosts, users, ports, keys, and jump hosts.
Documents remote command execution patterns for one command or interactive sessions.
Covers file transfer examples using scp and rsync, including dry runs.
Explains local, remote, and dynamic port forwarding commands.
Includes key generation, ssh-agent, known_hosts, and debug verbosity examples.
limitations: It is a command reference, not an automated SSH client.
It does not validate host keys or credentials for the user.
It does not detect organization-specific SSH policies.
Examples must be adapted before use on production systems.

use_cases.title: Server Access Setup
use_cases.description: Prepare repeatable SSH commands and config entries for routine server access.

use_cases.title: Secure File Transfer
use_cases.description: Choose scp or rsync patterns for moving files between local and remote systems.

use_cases.title: Tunnel Troubleshooting
use_cases.description: Build SSH tunnel commands for local services, remote services, and bastion hosts.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic SSH Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me build an SSH command for user [user] on host [host] using port [port]. Explain each option briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Draft an SSH Config Entry
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an SSH config entry for alias [alias], host [hostname], user [user], port [port], and identity file [key_path].

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Safe File Sync
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an rsync command to sync [local_path] to [remote_user]@[remote_host]:[remote_path]. Include a dry-run command first.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Bastion Tunnel
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an SSH command that connects through bastion [jump_host] to reach [internal_host] and forwards local port [local_port] to [remote_service].

output_examples.input: Request: connect to a server on a nonstandard port with a specific key.
output_examples.output: The answer provides an SSH command with a port option, an identity file option, and a short explanation of each placeholder.

output_examples.input: Request: move a directory to a remote server safely.
output_examples.output: The answer recommends an rsync dry run first, then a transfer command with archive, verbose, compression, and progress options.

output_examples.input: Request: reach an internal database through a bastion host.
output_examples.output: The answer describes a jump-host tunnel pattern and reminds the user to confirm hostnames, ports, and access policy.

best_practices: Use placeholders first, then replace hosts, users, paths, and ports after review.
Prefer Ed25519 keys and protect private keys with passphrases.
Run rsync dry runs before changing remote files.
anti_patterns: Do not copy commands against production hosts without checking every argument.
Do not add host keys to known_hosts without verifying fingerprints.
Do not enable agent forwarding or tunnels unless policy allows them.

faq.question: Does this skill run SSH commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides Markdown examples that a user may adapt and run manually.

faq.question: Can it manage private keys?
faq.answer: It shows key-related commands, but users remain responsible for key storage, passphrases, and access control.

faq.question: Does it support jump hosts?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for single and multiple jump hosts using SSH options and config entries.

faq.question: Does it cover file transfer?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes scp and rsync examples for uploads, downloads, recursive copies, and dry runs.

faq.question: Is SOCKS proxy support included?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents dynamic forwarding, which should be used only when allowed by network policy.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk medium?
faq.answer: The content is documentation, but the commands affect SSH credentials, trust stores, remote systems, and network routing.
